notes.anchor: my own estate was diminished by a law of Caesar's
notes.note: Matius means he suffered financially under one of Caesar's economic measures (probably the law on debts and interest of 49/48 BC), pointing out that he gained no material reward from Caesar's victory.

Matius quotes back, with bitter irony, the self-description of the Liberators (Caesar's assassins), who claimed to have restored Rome's freedom; he charges that they now deny others even the freedom to grieve.

notes.anchor: the games which the young Caesar held for Caesar's victory
notes.note: The ludi Victoriae Caesaris of July 44 BC, staged by Octavian to honour the dead dictator; the comet seen during them was taken as Caesar's soul ascending. Matius's supervision of them was the chief charge against him.

notes.anchor: I shall spend what remains of my life in peace at Rhodes
notes.note: Rhodes was the conventional retreat for a Roman seeking honourable withdrawal from politics into Greek philosophical leisure; Matius offers it as proof that he covets no power at Rome.
